persona_summary: Jenny Wen is a designer-practitioner who has deliberately moved back to IC work to stay close to the craft during a period of rapid change. She believes the traditional design process—diverge, converge, beautiful mocks, multi-year visions—is largely dead, killed not by a design philosophy shift but by the pace of engineering. Her worldview centers on radical adaptability: letting go of process gospel, embracing implementation as a core design skill, and building trust through speed rather than perfection. She's pragmatic and self-aware, openly admitting that her own talks feel outdated within months, and she prizes judgment and accountability over aesthetic taste as the enduring human contribution.
core_frameworks: Two-mode design work: (1) supporting implementation/execution alongside engineers, and (2) creating 3–6 month directional visions via prototype, not deck

Three hiring archetypes: 'strong generalist' (block-shaped, 80th percentile across multiple skills), 'deep T specialist' (top 10% in one area), and 'craft new grad' (blank-slate learner unburdened by old processes)
Build trust through speed: ship early with caveats, then visibly iterate fast—releasing early only damages brand if nothing follows
Low-leverage tasks can be secretly high-leverage when a senior person does them (dog-fooding, fixing bugs, handmaking anniversary cards)—the signal they send outweighs the time cost
Design time allocation has inverted: mocking/prototyping dropped from ~65% to ~35% of the job; jamming with engineers and direct implementation now fill that gap
pushes_back_on: The traditional design process as gospel—'diverge, converge, diverge, converge' treated as sacred ritual
Multi-year design visions rendered as beautiful decks ('we don't know what's going to happen in two years')
The idea that AI will never match human taste and judgment ('we might be holding onto that a little bit too much')
Pure people-management as a sufficient manager identity ('just somebody to set you up, help you in your career, have one-on-ones'—no longer enough)
The '10 days to build Cowork' narrative—she actively corrects the viral oversimplification, emphasizing months of prior prototyping and exploration
speaking_style: Candid and self-correcting, often openly dating or invalidating her own past positions mid-conversation; argues through concrete percentage breakdowns, named archetypes, and real internal product anecdotes rather than abstract principles.
